The surgeon lifts and pushes towards the sacrum, aspirates, and performs a drop test. Gas is introduced, and the single-digit pressure confirms correct placement. Approximately half a liter of gas is insufflated before the abdominal wall is released.

The surgeon confirms the placement of the trocar in the supraumbilical region, approximately two inches above the umbilicus. The rectus sheath is pierced with notable force due to its strength, commonly observed in infertility patients. The scope is cleaned and inserted, followed by the initiation of insufflation gas.

The patient is positioned in a steep head-low (Trendelenburg) position, allowing the bowel to shift towards the diaphragm. The surgeon inspects the appendicular area, noting the presence of adhesions, while confirming that the liver appears normal.

The surgeon confirms the fixed port placement with two ports on their side and one on the assistant's side. The lower port is positioned one inch above and medial to the anterior superior iliac spine. The surgeon visually identifies colitic adhesions and avoids them during the procedure.

The surgeon visually inspects the uterine tubes to identify the location of fibroids. The left tube is visible, and a large fibroid is noted on the posterior wall. The right tube is also visible, with a large fibroid on the anterior wall.
The surgeon examines the uterus, identifying a corneal fibroid with the fallopian tube visible nearby. There are three small fibroids, two large fibroids, and one seedling fibroid present.

The surgeon uses a vasopressor injection needle to inject into the anterior myometrium, penetrating half an inch into the tissue. The needle is aspirated before injecting 6 to 10 ml of vasopressor solution into the myometrium.
The surgeon injects a vasoconstrictor into the myometrium, with approximately fifty ml administered, ensuring the injection is not entering a venous sinus or vessel.

Depending on the appearance, then we decide... Pulse is very comfortable. In between also, I do not mind taking a look and reaffirming that pulse is fine. So, may be around three hundred to three fifty ml should be sufficient for this case. I am not likely to put the full four hundred ml. Eight, nine centimeter fibroid is when I require the complete four hundred ml.

The surgeon observes the uterus, noting that 300 ml of vasopressin has been injected, causing the upper fibroid to appear white. The anterior wall injection is affecting the posterior myometrium, preparing for the removal of the posterior fibroid with visible effects of vasopressin and aqua dissection.

The surgeon prepares to suture the uterine wall after removing fibroids, noting the distorted contour of the uterus due to the fibroids. The effect of the vasodilator is minimal on the posterior wall and fibroid.

The surgeon makes an elliptical incision on the uterus to facilitate effective suturing. The fibroid appears degenerated, with visible myometrial hypertrophy. Bleeding is present from the posterior wall, and the surgeon prepares to remove the fibroid before starting suturing.

The surgeon identifies a larger fibroid on the anterior side of the uterus, with saline visibly surrounding it. The fibroid is not trimmed, as the broad ligament tissue available for suturing decreases once the fibroid is delivered.

The surgeon uses a myoma screw to apply traction to a larger fibroid, facilitating its removal. The tooth needle holder provides counter traction, making the dissection plane more accessible for trimming.

The surgeon advances the myoma screw into the fibroid, successfully removing it. The uterus appears normal post-removal. The myoma screw is slightly bent due to the force applied, and the surgeon adjusts it to restore its shape.

The surgeon observes no bleeding anteriorly, but significant bleeding posteriorly, prompting the decision to cauterize. A small incision is made on the posterior uterine wall to remove the fibroid, avoiding a tunneling incision to minimize myometrial trauma.

The surgeon observes hypertrophy of the anterior fibroid and notes the posterior fibroid with approximately 50 ml of blood loss. A double needle suture, 24 by 4 cm, is prepared using a barb suture from Stratafix by Johnson & Johnson.

The surgeon begins suturing the posterior uterine wall, followed by the anterior wall, using a needle that is partially outside the abdominal wall. The sutures are placed through the full thickness of the uterine wall, avoiding excessive depth. The surgeon plans to locate and address a smaller fibroid on the anterior wall after completing the suturing.

The surgeon performs a double-layer closure on the uterine wall, using a suture to retract the tissue. The needle holder aids in pulling the tissue upward, allowing for a full-thickness bite. The deeper layer of the closure is already completed.

The surgeon performs a baseball stitch closure on the upper part of the incision, turning the needle inside out to pass it through the tissue gently. The suture line is completed with precision, ensuring the needle exits at the desired location before finishing the closure and returning to the incision line.

The surgeon performs a figure-of-eight stitch, turning the wrist to achieve the necessary grip for a deeper stitch. The suture is passed through the tissue, completing the stitch before returning to the inside-out technique.

The surgeon adjusts the tension of the suture thread to prevent excessive lifting of the tissue, ensuring better visualization for closing the area. Two additional suture bites are placed to complete the final closure.

The surgeon observes that the suture is marginally cut through due to excessive tightening and adjusts the tension gently. The posterior valve suturing is completed, and the surgeon uses scissors to cut off the excess thread.
The surgeon removes one needle and pulls in the second needle to continue tying the sutures, preparing to complete the anterior suturing of the surgical site.

The surgeon assesses the anterior part of the incision, noting the effect of vasopressin as there is no bleeding. The incision has shifted to a vertical orientation after fibroid removal, which may complicate closure compared to preferred oblique or horizontal incisions.
The surgical field is clearly visible with improved tissue exposure. The surgeon notes that trimming the tissue would have been beneficial, but the current state does not pose a significant issue.

The surgeon completes the first layer of closure after opening the bladder, noting the tough tissue consistency during suturing. The surgical field shows the effects of vasopressin injection, with visible tissue manipulation and suturing techniques applied to the adenomyotic focus.

The surgeon prepares to take a baseball stitch on a superficial layer of the uterine tissue. The difficulty arises in choosing between using a hand for a reverse stitch or twisting the uterine area to take a bite.

The surgeon takes a suture bite from the right side, demonstrating the technique of inside-out suturing. The left-hand bite requires comfort with left-hand suturing, as the suture is placed deep into the tissue.

The surgeon takes two more suturing bites on the uterine tissue, preparing to tie the thread despite its short length. The anterior and posterior sutures are completed, and the surgeon considers searching for another anterior fibroid.

Three fibroids have been removed, and the surgeon uses a myoma screw and Grasperani wash to manage the surgical field. Blood is visible in a peritoneal fold, which appears stable. The surgeon assesses the anterior area and considers the incision size needed for further removal of small fibroids.

The surgeon injects the remaining solution into the uterus, causing the tissue to turn white. The solution leaks from the cut open myometrium. A transverse incision is made in the uterine tissue, with plans to use figure-of-eight sutures for closure, aiming to remove the fibroid.

The surgeon identifies a very small seedling fibroid on the anterior uterine wall, similar to a previously noted posterior fibroid. A figure-of-eight suture is applied using a 2-0 Vicryl to close the incision on the anterior wall, without opening a barb suture.

The surgeon uses a bipolar device to coagulate tissue without suturing deeply, opting for two figure-of-eight sutures for closure. A cutting needle is used for better control, noting the difference from a barbed suture as it does not remain tight. The surgeon assesses if one more suture bite is needed.

The surgeon takes the last bite with a barbed suture, carefully tightening it step by step to ensure it remains secure without cutting through the tissue. The suture line is visible, and the tissue appears to blanch and become white as the suture is tightened.

The surgeon uses a vicryl suture to secure the superficial suture line with three or four bites. The barbed suture technique is noted for its effectiveness in maintaining tension. Bipolar cautery is applied for surface coagulation of the seedling fibroid, ensuring the deeper myometrium remains unaffected. A quick wash is performed before proceeding with morcellation.

The surgical field shows mild oozing from the incision site, but it is not significant enough to require a drain or additional sutures. The anterior aspect appears stable without further intervention.
The surgeon removes blood from the pouch of Douglas, assessing the amount of residual blood post-morcellation. The uterine tissue appears clumped and hypertrophic due to extensive suturing.

The surgeon uses an instrument to grasp and extract a long strip of tissue, ensuring the prongs close properly to avoid damaging the blade. The extracted tissue has a consistent texture, neither too soft nor too hard.

The surgeon uses a morcellator to cut and remove tissue, pulling on the grasper to facilitate the cutting process. The procedure is expected to take around forty minutes, after which the effect of vasopressin will have diminished, allowing for a true assessment of hemostasis.

The surgeon completes the morcellation and removes the morcellator from the surgical field. A thumb is placed on the port to prevent leakage, and a 5mm port is reinserted. The surgeon assesses the fluid collected in the pouch, noting it is more fluid than blood. There is some oozing observed, which may cause peritoneal irritation and shoulder pain.

The surgeon observes the surgical field, noting that there is no significant blood accumulation in the upper areas, and any small collection has settled in the pouch. The surgeon prepares to give a head-high position and wash to clear smaller tissue fragments towards the pelvis. The appendix is visually confirmed to be normal, without signs of appendicitis or adhesion.
The surgeon observes a minimal ooze from the needle puncture site at the end of suturing, noting that it is a mild trickle and deciding not to place a drain for this case.

The surgeon removes the cannula from a 5mm puncture site and inserts a Ryle's tube as a drain. Ten cc of ropivacaine is injected into the port site and another ten cc into the peritoneal cavity, with additional injection above the peritoneum and below the muscle.
The needle is inserted almost directly inside, visibly poking through just above the peritoneum, allowing the sap or ropy vacuum to be introduced into this space.
The surgeon prepares to close the morcellator port by placing the morcellator and carefully inserting a needle to take bites, avoiding the muscle tissue.

It will be tightened after the abdomen has collapsed. But if you are poking the needle inside, you have to place the needle with the insufflation on. If you are using that C-shaped portoche needle from above, then you need not inflate and then do it. Either you can deflate.
The surgeon uses 2-0 vicryl suture to knot the muscle, taking care not to strangulate the tissue during the stitching process.
The surgeon uses skin staplers to close the skin incision after completing the myomectomy procedure.
